SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper provides clear mathematical definitions of the model (e.g., M1 in Eq.1, M2 in Eq.3, the CNN pipeline in Eq.4–5 and the learning objective in Eq.8) and supports its main claims with large-scale experiments on Ubuntu and Douban corpora. The experimental methodology includes proper baselines, ablation (Table 4), visualization, and statistical significance testing (t-test p ≤ 0.01), which strengthens the evidence. Minor concerns remain about some implementation/retrieval choices (a heuristic tf-idf retrieval step is used and details of negative sampling are brief), but these do not undermine the main conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a novel ‘matching-first’ architecture that matches a response with each utterance via multi-granularity similarity matrices and then accumulates matching vectors sequentially with an RNN — a non-trivial combination not present in the cited prior work (e.g., Lowe et al., Zhou et al., MV-LSTM). The explicit use of 2D similarity channels (word-word and sequence-sequence) fed to a CNN per utterance and then chronologically aggregated is a distinctive design that meaningfully extends existing matching/hierarchical approaches.

CLARITY.justification: The method section is formal and gives equations for all key components (GRU, similarity matrices, CNN, aggregation, attention), which aids understanding. However, the provided text contains duplicated and fragmented paragraphs and some editorial artifacts, making parts of the exposition harder to follow; a cleaner presentation and more explicit reproduction details (hyperparameters of CNN layers, pooling sizes) would improve clarity.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experimental evaluation is substantial: two datasets (Ubuntu and Douban), many baselines (single-turn and multi-turn), ablation studies (ReplaceM, ReplaceA, single-channel), visualization, context-length analysis, and a human comparison with a generation model. Limitations and retrieval-stage choices are only briefly discussed (heuristic tf-idf expansion), and while enough detail for a high-level reproduction is present (word2vec init, Adam, GPU), some exact architectural/hyperparameter details are omitted.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem — multi-turn response selection in retrieval-based chatbots — and demonstrates significant empirical gains over strong baselines on standard corpora, suggesting researchers and practitioners will adopt or build upon the approach. The method opens clear avenues for further work (better retrieval, alternative aggregation), though it is an evolution rather than a transformative paradigm shift.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (multi-turn response selection) and methods (neural matching, CNNs over similarity matrices, RNN aggregation) are squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics (Rn@k, MAP, MRR, human eval) meet community standards and target the right audience.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Comparisons include a comprehensive set of baselines (TF-IDF, RNN/CNN/LSTM/BiLSTM, MV-LSTM, Match-LSTM, Multi-View, DL2R) and both ablation and statistical significance tests are reported. The paper analyzes why prior single-turn concatenation or heuristic reformulation methods underperform and provides targeted ablations (e.g., ReplaceM, ReplaceA) that clarify the gains from the proposed components.
